blocks.text: Then Moses went up from the plains of Moab to Mount Nebo, to the top of Pisgah, which is opposite Jericho, and the Lord showed him the whole land: Gilead as far as Dan,

1 : , responding to the command of 32.49; Num 27.12. The verse joins two different traditions about the site of Moses’s death: , in Transjordan, east of Jericho; and , which is slightly to its northwest, and unmentioned in 32.49. To preserve both traditions, the editor presents them as if they were the same.
all Naphtali, the land of Ephraim and Manasseh, all the land of Judah as far as the Western Sea,
the Negeb, and the Plain—that is, the valley of Jericho, the city of palm trees—as far as Zoar.

2–3 : The lofty vantage point allows Moses to look northward to the Sea of Galilee (area of the tribal allotment of Dan and ), to the (the Mediterranean), south to the desert and along the Jordan rift valley as far south as (once located at the southern end of the Dead Sea as one of the “cities of the Plain”; Gen 14.2,8; 19.29).

The Lord said to him, “This is the land of which I swore to Abraham, to Isaac, and to Jacob, saying, ‘I will give it to your descendants’; I have let you see it with your eyes, but you shall not cross over there.”
Then Moses, the servant of the Lord, died there in the land of Moab, at the Lord’s command.
5 : , see 32.50n. The unusual formulation greatly honors Moses, who, despite advanced age, does not die of old age nor succumb to physical or intellectual infirmity.
He was buried in a valley in the land of Moab, opposite Beth‐peor, but no one knows his burial place to this day.

6 : , lit., “He buried him,” a clear indication that God buried Moses, as God sealed Noah into the ark (Gen 7.16). Instead of Moses’s progeny assuming the responsibility of caring for the dead, God undertook it personally. , 3.29; 4.46; Josh 13.20. , thus precluding pilgrimages to the site as a shrine.
Moses was one hundred twenty years old when he died; his sight was unimpaired and his vigor had not abated.
The Israelites wept for Moses in the plains of Moab thirty days; then the period of mourning for Moses was ended. What Moses saw from Mt. Nebo.
8 : , so also for Aaron (Num 20.29).
Joshua son of Nun was full of the spirit of wisdom, because Moses had laid his hands on him; and the Israelites obeyed him, doing as the Lord had commanded Moses.

9 : , as at 1.13 and 16.18–20, Deuteronomy revises earlier traditions to stress wisdom as the essential qualification of office, and thus what Joshua receives from Moses. Num 27.18 depicts Joshua as already possessing an undefined “spirit” (often associated with prophecy or possession), while Moses was to transfer his “authority” to him. , as at Num 27.22–23, a means of transfer of attributes (Lev 16.21; Num 8.10–13), here used for investiture into office.
Never since has there arisen a prophet in Israel like Moses, whom the Lord knew face to face.

10 : , more correctly, “But there never again arose in Israel a prophet like Moses.” NRSV obscures the tension between the perspective of this verse and the divine promise to Moses that the line of prophetic succession will continue in the future: “I will raise up for them a prophet like you” (18.18). , rather than through dreams or visions (13.1); similarly, Ex 33.11; Num 12.8–10. Other traditions reject the concept that Moses had such direct access to God (Ex 33.20–23).
He was unequaled for all the signs and wonders that the Lord sent him to perform in the land of Egypt, against Pharaoh and all his servants and his entire land,
and for all the mighty deeds and all the terrifying displays of power that Moses performed in the sight of all Israel.

34.1–12: The death of Moses. This chapter directly continues the narrative concerning the death of Moses and the transfer of leadership to Joshua, his successor (ch 31 and 32.48–52). At another level, however, it also continues Num 27, where God had commanded Moses to “go up this mountain” to survey the promised land before his death, and to “lay your hand” upon Joshua, to transfer the mantle of authority to him (vv. 12,18), bracketing Deuteronomy and suggesting that editors interrupted an earlier form of the narrative in order to include Deuteronomy in the Pentateuch.

10–12 : Moses as the greatest exemplar of prophecy both in direct access to divine revelation and in power to work miracles. The double elevation, which differs from his more human representation elsewhere in the book, suggests an editor’s later, idealizing retrospective, with Deuteronomy now worked into the Pentateuch as a whole.
